stem: A automotive testing study measures the fuel consumption (L/100km) for a sample of 30 vehicles. The sample mean is 271.6 L/100km with a sample standard deviation of 45.7 L/100km. However, 2 of the 30 observations appear to be outliers (values more than 2.5 standard deviations from the mean). If these outliers are removed, the remaining 28 observations have a mean of 254.9 L/100km and standard deviation of 34.7 L/100km. Estimate the true population mean and provide a 95% confidence interval that accounts for the uncertainty about whether outliers should be included.

ground_truth.answer: With all data: mean = 271.6, 95% CI = [254.5, 288.7]. Without outliers: mean = 254.9, 95% CI = [241.8, 268.0]. Combined interval accounting for analytic uncertainty: [241.8, 288.7]. Point estimate: 263.2 L/100km.

solution_steps: The outlier treatment choice creates structural uncertainty. Combined interval: [241.8, 288.7].
common_failure_modes: Using only the full sample without considering outlier impact
Removing outliers without reporting both analyses
Not recognizing that the choice of analysis method is itself a source of uncertainty
